video_title: Can $500 Billion Win the AI Race? | Anton Leicht
gist: Carnegie Endowment fellow Anton Leicht argues that middle powers (Europe, Japan, South Korea, etc.) face a default future of capturing all of frontier AI's risk and none of its benefit unless they pursue a compute-for-access strategy with US labs; he separately sketches, and largely dismisses as unlikely, an allied $500 billion four-year coalition project to build an independent frontier AGI model, while also covering AI taxation, labor-market policy, soft nationalization of US labs, and the pause/slowdown debate.

insights.claim: Countries that do not build their own frontier AI models default into capturing all the societal risk that comes from these models spreading through society while capturing almost none of the economic benefit, according to Anton Leicht.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Frames the baseline policy problem for every non-US, non-China government: passive non-participation in the AI supply chain is itself a strategic loss, not a neutral position.

insights.claim: Leicht's recommended strategy for middle powers to secure reliable AI access is compute-for-access: build data centres that absorb US labs' inference-capacity crunch, and in exchange negotiate guaranteed frontier-model access at parity with the US commercial market.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: A concrete, testable framework for evaluating national AI deals: watch whether new data-centre buildouts in allied countries come bundled with explicit model-access-parity clauses.

insights.claim: Leicht estimates the realistic benchmark labs need to consider diversifying inference compute outside the US is a data centre built in about 18 to 24 months, ideally offering something like a gigawatt of capacity within one to two years.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Gives investors a concrete timeline/scale benchmark for judging whether a given national data-centre project is fast enough to actually earn access-parity leverage with frontier labs.

insights.claim: Direct government-to-government AI deals are fragile: the UK-US Technology Prosperity Deal, which included a Stargate data-centre buildout pillar, unravelled after the US inserted unrelated food-safety demands into negotiations and OpenAI partly reneged on the Stargate component citing copyright and energy-price concerns.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Warns investors and policymakers that headline US bilateral tech-cooperation deals can be renegotiated or partly abandoned via unrelated political leverage, making them a weak basis for long-term AI-access planning.

insights.claim: Leicht argues deals struck directly with AI labs are more durable than deals struck with the US government, because labs that have sunk billions into a country's data centre gain a commercial incentive to lobby the US government against export restrictions that would strand that investment.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Suggests investors should track lab-to-country commercial commitments (data-centre buildouts, access-parity contracts) as a more reliable predictor of durable AI market access than government treaties.
insights.quote: if the US government decides to come up with some access restriction, then the American firms that are involved in these deals... now have an incentive to tell the US government... can you at least give a carveout to this country

insights.claim: The UAE's chip-access deal and Norway's original Stargate arrangement (now effectively a Microsoft buildout after OpenAI partly walked back its commitment) both illustrate that early compute deals were struck from weak bargaining positions before frontier-access anxiety became acute, and neither secured genuine access-parity guarantees.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Signals that early-mover AI infrastructure deals in the Gulf and Nordics should not be read as templates for future access-parity negotiations; later entrants may need to negotiate harder terms.

insights.claim: The Fable export-control episode, reported by Wired as tied to Fable's access being extended to South Korea's SK Telecom over alleged China ties, has accelerated Washington's policy conversation toward export-controlling AI models (weights and/or outputs) much faster than Leicht expected, compressing the window middle powers have to set up compute-for-access arrangements.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: A concrete near-term catalyst: watch for accelerated US export-control rulemaking on model weights/outputs, which would tighten the timeline for any middle power still negotiating access-parity deals.
insights.quote: The Fable thing — even if it is a red herring — has shaken up a lot of people, and has made a lot of people more worried about this.

insights.claim: Leicht's own back-of-envelope estimate is that a coalition of middle powers pooling talent, compute, funding, and supply-chain leverage could build a competitive frontier AI model for roughly $500 billion over a four-year project.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Sets a concrete, named price tag for an independent non-US/non-China frontier-AI effort that investors and policymakers can use as a reference point when new sovereign AI initiatives are announced.

insights.claim: Leicht argues $500 billion is not an absurd sum if the best version of an allied liberal-democracy coalition — the EU plus Canada, the UK, Australia and Japan and South Korea — pooled resources, since it would be affordable for that combined economic bloc.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Frames the feasibility question as political willingness rather than raw affordability, which is the key variable for investors tracking whether a rival non-US frontier lab could actually get funded.

insights.claim: Leicht's proposed structure for a coalition frontier lab is to replicate the standard frontier-lab organizational model exactly (pay top engineers heavily, give them massive compute, minimal outside interference) rather than reinvent it, funded by irrevocable upfront capital commitments from every member government routed through a small number of designated national liaisons.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: A concrete organizational blueprint that would signal a serious (vs. symbolic) state-backed AI effort if it appeared in real policy proposals.
insights.quote: You just need to reinvent the wheel... In fact, you mustn’t reinvent the wheel in any of this.

insights.claim: Leicht's preferred coalition membership is the EU, UK, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, South Korea, and Japan (Taiwan if achievable), but he expects the UK, South Korea, and Japan to opt out initially because their current bilateral relationships with the US look like a better bet than joining an EU-led coalition with a poor track record of delivering announced joint projects.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Predicts a narrower, EU-Canada-Australia-anchored coalition as the realistic starting membership, useful for tracking which countries' policy moves would actually signal this project advancing.

insights.claim: Leicht frames the coalition frontier-model project economically like a warplane or aircraft carrier — a strategic asset governments buy without expecting it to pay for itself — rather than a commercial venture, distinguishing it from US labs which must justify returns to private capital markets that do not exist at scale outside the US.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Reframes how to value a state-backed AI project: judge it against defense-budget logic, not venture-style ROI, when assessing whether treasuries will actually fund one.

insights.claim: Leicht is pessimistic that a successful European frontier model would translate into strong international commercial market share, noting Europe has historically found it harder to build and sell a winning software product than to build large infrastructure, even though Mistral retains a decent US market share today.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Tempers expectations that a state-funded European frontier model would become commercially self-sustaining or a genuine Nvidia/OpenAI/Anthropic-style competitor even if technically successful.

insights.claim: Leicht's base case is that the $500 billion coalition project does not happen, primarily because political awareness in national treasuries lags the required urgency by months, causing the effective price tag to keep rising — from $500 billion today to an estimated $600 billion in six months, and toward roughly $1 trillion after further delay.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: A high-conviction call that a state-backed rival to US frontier labs is unlikely near-term; if unexpected coalition funding news emerges, it would materially update this base case.
insights.quote: So $500 billion is a price tag now. If we convince people to do it, we’re going to have to tell them in six months that now it costs $600 billion.

insights.claim: Leicht's rough estimate for the compute a coalition frontier project would need is on the order of 3 million Blackwell-class chips — more than are currently available to purchase given Nvidia's limited supply — making chip acquisition, not just funding, a binding constraint.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Quantifies the hardware bottleneck for any non-US frontier effort; a useful reference point for judging whether announced sovereign AI compute deals are anywhere close to frontier scale.
insights.quote: something like 3 million Blackwell-class chips for building this thing

insights.claim: Leicht notes much of a frontier lab's effective talent base today consists of coding agents rather than only human engineers, so a coalition project without access to leading tools like Codex or Claude Code would be materially slower to build a competitive model, and the gap would widen the longer that access is denied.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Identifies coding-agent access as an underappreciated AI-competitiveness bottleneck alongside chips — worth tracking as a leading indicator of whether any non-US lab can realistically catch up.
insights.quote: much of the talent base of frontier labs today are already coding agents

insights.claim: Leicht expects Nvidia to be commercially motivated to sell chips broadly to diversify its buyer base, but expects the US government to resist Nvidia selling at scale to a coalition explicitly aiming for frontier parity, since that carries different security optics than routine commercial sales or even sales to China's diffusion-stage buyers.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Suggests chip-export policy risk for any coalition AI project is asymmetric: Nvidia's commercial incentives favor the deal, but US political/security incentives cut against it, making export-license politics a key catalyst to watch.

insights.claim: Leicht argues the main leverage point for a coalition to secure chips and coding-agent access is the semiconductor supply chain itself — particularly ASML in the Netherlands, plus Japanese and South Korean high-bandwidth-memory and semiconductor-equipment suppliers — since Nvidia and the US depend on these upstream inputs to build chips at all.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Identifies ASML and Japanese/Korean semiconductor-equipment suppliers as the practical geopolitical leverage points in any allied AI-access negotiation, relevant for assessing export-control risk around these firms.

insights.claim: Leicht believes US policymakers currently do not take a middle-power government-led AGI project seriously, expecting it to fail like past catch-up projects, which paradoxically means the US may not act early to block chip exports — creating a limited window for a coalition to get chips online before Washington reassesses the threat.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Implies a narrow, closing arbitrage window: a credible coalition project might acquire chips precisely because it is currently underestimated in DC, before political attention catches up.

themes.theme: Taxing and Cushioning the AI Transition

insights.claim: Leicht argues a US government equity stake in AI companies — an idea associated with Bernie Sanders and reportedly under White House consideration — is a bad policy because even a 10x-100x rise in AI-lab valuations would not come close to funding large-scale social disruption relief, since the broader economy dwarfs the labs even at that scale.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: A direct, high-conviction pushback against a live US policy proposal; investors should weight equity-stake-in-AI-labs proposals as economically insufficient even if politically popular.

insights.claim: Leicht warns that a US equity stake tied to AI-lab valuations (e.g., a Trump account funded with AI equity) would create a strong government incentive to under-regulate those labs, since tougher AI regulation would directly reduce the value of the government's own holdings.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Flags a specific conflict-of-interest mechanism worth monitoring: any formal US government equity stake in frontier AI firms should raise flags about weakened future safety regulation.

insights.claim: For non-US allies, Leicht argues the closest workable analogue to an AI-lab equity stake is a genuinely diversified sovereign wealth fund that includes AI labs as one hedge among many, rather than a narrow fund that only buys stakes in a single AI developer.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: A concrete design principle for any allied sovereign-AI-investment vehicle: diversification across the AI stack (or broader markets), not concentrated single-company exposure.
insights.quote: the stupid “I only buy Anthropic stock” version of the sovereign wealth fund

insights.claim: Leicht prefers raising the corporate income tax over a narrow AI-usage 'token tax' (which would blunt AI adoption and competitiveness) or a broad consumption tax (which is politically toxic because it appears as a visible line-item, e.g. on a food-delivery order, even though most of its burden actually falls on companies).
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete policy preference to watch for: corporate-tax-rate proposals are the more likely funding mechanism for AI-transition safety nets than usage-based AI taxes.

insights.claim: Leicht expects a massive revenue crisis for treasuries broadly as economic activity shifts from heavily taxed human labour toward lightly taxed capital and AI agents, estimating that naively moving one unit of revenue from labour to capital could lose half or more of the associated tax revenue given current corporate tax rates (~25%) and capital investment tax breaks.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: A quantified warning about a structural fiscal shock as AI displaces labour income; relevant for anticipating future corporate tax hikes or new capital-taxation regimes across developed economies.

insights.claim: Leicht argues capital-flight risk from AI-heavy firms is more limited than feared because compute-intensive firms remain physically anchored to data centres, meaning a coordinated 10-15% minimum corporate tax floor across allied countries could still capture meaningful revenue despite firms' nominal mobility.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Suggests physical data-centre location will remain a durable tax-revenue anchor even as firms become more digitally 'mobile,' informing expectations around future minimum-tax coordination among allies.

insights.claim: Leicht makes the counterintuitive recommendation that middle powers should make labour markets more flexible, not more rigid, as AI disruption arrives, because protecting current jobs risks a much worse later outcome (mass layoffs and higher structural unemployment) once protected firms get outcompeted by more AI-adaptive domestic or foreign rivals.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: A specific, actionable labor-policy call that runs against the intuitive protectionist response to AI job displacement; useful for evaluating which countries' labor reforms are AI-transition-compatible.
insights.quote: you have to bite the bullet on the counterintuitive conclusion of making your labour market more flexible as the AI disruption comes in

insights.claim: Leicht proposes 'junior job subsidies' — paying firms to keep early-career workers employed alongside AI agents — as a bridge measure, distinguishing this cohort from older displaced industrial workers because young workers want career progression rather than just wage replacement, making simple wage or job guarantees insufficient to prevent political backlash.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: A concrete, named policy mechanism to watch for as governments design AI-transition social safety nets targeted at early-career workers specifically.

insights.claim: Leicht expects AI-driven job disruption to be politically blamed on AI regardless of how well-designed the actual safety net is, especially when it coincides with weak macro sentiment, favoring the political appeal of restrictive policies (token taxes, human-in-the-loop mandates) that would slow AI adoption and further erode competitiveness.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Anticipates a political feedback loop where economic anxiety produces anti-AI-adoption policy that then worsens the competitiveness problem it was meant to solve — a risk factor for firms operating in jurisdictions prone to this dynamic.

themes.theme: Manufacturing as the New AGI Moat

insights.claim: Leicht's core near-term industrial policy recommendation is aggressive foreign direct investment screening, citing Bezos's new company Prometheus buying up manufacturing capacity as an example of outside actors moving to acquire undervalued industrial assets before their owners realize how valuable those assets will become once integrated with AI-driven robotics.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: A concrete, actionable policy call (FDI screening on manufacturing assets) and a specific company (Prometheus/Bezos) to watch as a bellwether of outside capital moving early on the AI-manufacturing thesis.

insights.claim: Leicht argues that owning AI-compatible manufacturing capacity — factories with sensors and feedback loops that can report performance data back to a directing AI model — may become as scarce and valuable an asset as the frontier AI model itself, because building such a factory is nearly as hard as building the model.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Identifies AI-integrable manufacturing capacity as an underpriced strategic asset class, relevant for investors evaluating industrial/robotics companies as potential AGI-economy bottleneck plays.
insights.quote: it’s almost as hard to build a factory that can do this as it is to build an AI model that can do that

insights.claim: Leicht warns that selling industrial assets today at a price that looks like a windfall risks giving away decades of future AGI-economy revenue, since buyers with better foresight about AI's trajectory can offer prices that look generous today but represent a steep discount to future value.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: A direct warning to owners of industrial/manufacturing assets in middle-power countries against selling to well-capitalized foreign buyers without pricing in AI-driven future value.
insights.quote: this would have been your slice of the light cone: this is a super important piece of manufacturing; this would have secured your stake in the AGI economy for decades to come

insights.claim: Leicht lists specific steps to make manufacturing 'AI compatible' and thus attractive to frontier labs for cooperation: modularize production to react quickly to AI-directed changes, keep a workforce skilled enough to implement ad hoc modifications, avoid regulating away data-sharing with AI developers, and instrument plants with sensors that feed performance data back to the model.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: A practical checklist for industrial policymakers or manufacturers seeking to position their facilities as attractive AI-lab cooperation partners rather than acquisition targets.

themes.theme: Soft Nationalization of US AI Labs

insights.claim: Leicht's mainline scenario is that the US does not formally nationalize AI labs via executive order or legislation; instead it exerts control informally through government-linked observers embedded in labs, feedback loops to the executive branch, and implicit threats to use tools like export controls or the Defense Production Act.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Sets an expectation that US government control over frontier labs will show up as informal pressure and negotiated concessions rather than a single visible regulatory event — useful for interpreting future lab policy shifts.

insights.claim: Leicht expects ongoing, ad hoc 'messy negotiations' between the US administration and AI labs (citing the Fable episode as an example of this pattern) rather than a comprehensive nationalization framework, eventually producing only minimal executive orders that standardize parts of an otherwise informal back-and-forth.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Predicts continued regulatory unpredictability for AI labs operating in the US, with policy emerging through case-by-case negotiation rather than clear statutory rules — a relevant uncertainty factor for AI-lab investors.

insights.claim: Leicht observes that the open policy debate ecosystem (what Dean Ball called the 'republic of letters' during the SB-1047 discussion) is being starved as more people with the best information move from public writing into inside-government or inside-lab roles, making external, credible analysis both scarcer and more necessary.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Flags a growing information asymmetry: outside observers should expect less public visibility into frontier AI developments over time as expert discussion migrates behind closed doors inside labs and governments.

themes.theme: Pause, Slowdown, and the China Question

insights.claim: Leicht is skeptical of an AI 'pause,' arguing that the politically realistic coalition assembled to push for one (groups opposed to AI for unrelated reasons like jobs or environmental concerns) is unlikely to stay together long enough to negotiate the sophisticated international treaty a real pause would require, while a naive unilateral US pause would crash markets tied to AI infrastructure investment.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: A high-conviction case against a naive domestic AI pause as both politically fragile and financially destabilizing, relevant for assessing tail risk around pause-movement legislative proposals.
insights.quote: the first thing you do is you crash the stock market, because that is just super dependent on the infrastructure investments

insights.claim: Leicht distinguishes a 'pause' from a 'slowdown' that reallocates compute away from frontier R&D and toward inference, deployment, and societal hardening, viewing the slowdown version as more coordinated and potentially workable in principle.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Offers a more tractable near-term policy alternative to a hard pause that investors and policymakers could realistically see implemented, shifting compute demand from training toward inference infrastructure.

insights.claim: Leicht argues a symmetric global frontier-capability slowdown would be geopolitically asymmetric in practice, because China is not currently doing much frontier development and would disproportionately benefit from a slowdown while it catches up on semiconductor manufacturing and AI diffusion.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Explains why the US is unlikely to accept a frontier-training slowdown on its own terms, a key structural obstacle for any international AI-slowdown treaty proposal.

insights.claim: Leicht argues any credible US-China AI slowdown deal would need matched Chinese concessions on semiconductor indigenization and AI diffusion, not just frontier training limits, and considers verifying Chinese compliance on those broader industrial-policy fronts far harder than verifying a narrow training-compute cap.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Highlights verification of Chinese industrial policy (not just compute caps) as the binding constraint on any credible bilateral AI-slowdown treaty, a key risk factor for such negotiations succeeding.

insights.claim: Leicht considers a blanket 'ban on superintelligence' operationally meaningless since narrow AI systems are already superhuman at specific strategically relevant tasks like large-scale data and image analysis; he views banning closed recursive self-improvement (RSI) loops within labs as the more operationalizable and defensible policy target.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Reframes the AI-safety policy debate away from vague 'superintelligence bans' toward a specific, monitorable target (closed RSI loops), useful for evaluating the seriousness of future AI-safety legislative proposals.
insights.quote: this is like the lazy version of this “Do you want to ban calculators?” thing

insights.claim: Leicht says he would feel much more comfortable with a US 'decisive strategic advantage' in AI if it were constrained by both classic constitutional separation of powers and broader Western-alliance-level distribution of control, versus a scenario of tight amalgamation between labs and the US security apparatus with few checks, which he explicitly does not want to see.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Articulates a specific governance condition (alliance-level and constitutional checks) that would distinguish an acceptable vs. dangerous concentration of AI-driven geopolitical power — a lens for evaluating future US AI-security policy moves.
insights.quote: I’d feel a lot better about a Western alliance with a strategic advantage, with the DSA, than just an unconstrained US.

insights.claim: Leicht argues that broadening the set of actors with genuine leverage over powerful-AI deployment decisions (more middle powers able to withdraw compute or supply-chain inputs) narrows the range of possible outcomes and reduces loss-of-control risk driven by a single volatile actor making unconstrained decisions, even though it also constrains upside from bold unilateral moves.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Provides a structural argument for why distributing AI-related leverage across allies (not just the US) could be a net safety positive, relevant to debates about centralizing vs. distributing control over frontier AI deployment.

themes.theme: Country Winners and Losers in the AGI Economy

insights.claim: Leicht is bullish on Germany's long-run AI position specifically because its skilled workforce, large industrial (rather than service-heavy) economy, and existing bilateral relationships are the right assets to extract counter-leverage in a US-led AGI economy, contingent on Germany fixing generic, non-AI-specific problems like labour-market flexibility and returning to 3-4% growth.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: A specific country call: Germany's AI-era competitiveness hinges on generic domestic reforms (labor flexibility, growth) rather than AI-specific policy, useful for evaluating German economic-policy news through an AI lens.

insights.claim: Leicht is comparatively bearish on Singapore, Hong Kong, and even the UK for the AGI era specifically, because their heavily service-based economies are the segment most exposed to disruption by AI agents, meaning strong current economic performance does not predict resilience once AGI-level automation arrives.
insights.speaker: Anton Leicht
insights.so_what: Flags service-economy hubs (Singapore, Hong Kong, UK) as structurally more exposed to AI-driven disruption than their current strong growth numbers would suggest, a relevant risk factor for country/sector allocation.
